primary_concept: Consuming a free external API to populate app content dynamically
secondary_concepts: Understanding what an API is and why external data sources are useful
Differentiating free vs. paid APIs and when to use each
Recognizing common API failure modes: rate limits, data format changes, downtime
Storing game state with localStorage between sessions
mvp_features: Fetch trivia questions from the Open Trivia Database API (opentdb.com) by category and difficulty
Display one question at a time with multiple-choice answer options
Score tracking that increments on correct answers and shows a final score at the end
A start screen that lets the player choose a category or difficulty before the game begins
A results screen that shows the final score and a button to play again
stretch_features: Persist the player's high score across sessions using localStorage
Add a countdown timer per question to increase difficulty
Display a running leaderboard of past scores stored in localStorage
Show the correct answer with a brief explanation after each wrong answer
forbidden_features: User accounts or login — requires a database, which is not introduced until Module 2 and is out of scope for a free-API demo
Paid API integration (e.g., a premium trivia API with richer content) — paid APIs are introduced conceptually in Video 3.2 but not implemented until later work
LLM-generated questions or AI-powered hints — LLM API integration is introduced in Video 3.4, after this demo app is built
Saving question history to a JSON file or database — file-based and database persistence introduce backend complexity beyond what Module 3 demos require
Multiplayer or shared scoreboards — requires user identity and a database, both out of scope
Payment processing or premium content gating — explicitly identified in Video 3.5 as a ceiling case requiring a developer
learner_outcome: Learners will be able to integrate the Open Trivia Database free API into the Trivia Game App so that questions are fetched live from an external service rather than hardcoded, demonstrating how to connect an app to outside data without writing backend code.
instructor_talking_points: Narrate why hardcoding trivia questions would be impractical — this sets up the motivating problem that APIs solve before making the first fetch call.
Show the opentdb.com API docs briefly and explain that a URL with parameters is all it takes to get structured data back — demystifying what an API call actually is.
When the API returns data, pause to read the JSON shape aloud and explain how the coding agent maps that raw data onto the UI — making the invisible visible.
Demonstrate what happens when the API is temporarily unreachable or returns an error, and show the learner how to spot whether the bug is in their app or in the external service.
scope_warnings: Adding a persistent leaderboard with player names: this feels like a small feature but immediately requires a way to store and retrieve user identity, pulling in database concepts from Module 2.

Switching from opentdb.com to a richer paid trivia API mid-build: introduces API key security, billing setup, and environment variable handling — all of which belong in a dedicated paid-API lesson, not this demo.
Asking the coding agent to generate custom questions with an LLM: this is exactly the Module 3 Video 4 concept and should be reserved for that lesson or the capstone, not introduced here.
Building a timer that syncs across two players in real time: requires WebSockets or a shared backend state, far beyond the scope of a free-API integration demo.
Adding social sharing so players can post their score: requires OAuth or a third-party social API, which is a paid/authenticated API pattern outside this module's scope.
